items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.correct: Art that does not depict any recognisable object - exploring form, colour, and line directly.
items.misconception: Art that looks 'a bit like' something but not exactly.
items.random_wrong: Art that doesn't represent any country.
items.why: Students soften the term to 'stylised'. Non-representational means NO reference to objects at all - pure visual exploration (Rothko, Mondrian), distinct from semi-abstract work that simplifies recognisable forms.

items.correct: Strong contrast between light and dark to model three-dimensional form.
items.why: Students think it's about subject matter (night scenes). Chiaroscuro is a TECHNIQUE using value contrast to suggest volume - exemplified by Caravaggio but used since the Renaissance to model form.

items.q: Ways of Seeing was first broadcast in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1972. “1962” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Benjamin. “Greenberg” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Berger's series Ways of Seeing comprises 4 episodes; if each runs 30 minutes, total runtime in minutes is:
items.why: The real figure is 120. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
